# OpenLegion — Full Reference

## What is OpenLegion?
OpenLegion is a managed AI agent platform that provides container isolation, blind credential injection, budget controls, and multi-agent orchestration for production deployments. Users bring their own LLM API keys — no markup on model usage.

## Key Features
- Per-agent Docker container isolation with dedicated network namespaces
- Blind credential injection (agents never access raw API keys)
- Budget and cost governance per agent and per project
- Multi-agent orchestration with project-level organization
- Web dashboard to deploy, configure, and chat with agents
- Deterministic YAML DAG workflows for task routing
- 100+ LLM providers via LiteLLM
- Multi-channel deployment: Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, CLI, API
- Persistent Chrome + KasmVNC browser automation
- MCP-compatible tool extensibility
- Zero external dependencies (no Redis, no Kubernetes, no LangChain)

## Pricing
$19/month per user for a dedicated hosted instance. Bring your own LLM API keys. No markup on model usage. You pay LLM providers directly at their published rates.

## Architecture: Three-Zone Trust Model

Zone 1 — User Zone (Full Trust): CLI, Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, API. All inputs validated and sanitized.

Zone 2 — Mesh Host (Trusted Coordinator): FastAPI server running Blackboard (shared state via SQLite), PubSub message router, Credential Vault (vault proxy for blind injection), Orchestrator with permission matrix, Container Manager with cost tracking. Only component that touches API keys.

Zone 3 — Agent Containers (Untrusted): Each agent runs as its own FastAPI instance in a dedicated Docker container with its own /data volume, memory database, and strict resource caps (1GB RAM, 1 CPU, non-root, no-new-privileges). Even a fully compromised agent cannot access API keys, other agents' data, or the host system.

## Security — Six-Layer Defense

1. Runtime Isolation: Docker containers or Docker Sandbox microVMs per agent.
2. Container Hardening: Non-root user (UID 1000), no-new-privileges flag, memory and CPU resource limits.
3. Credential Separation: Vault proxy holds all API keys. Agents call through the proxy, never see secrets.
4. Permission Enforcement: Per-agent ACL matrix controls which tools, files, and mesh operations are allowed.
5. Input Validation: Path traversal prevention, safe condition evaluation, token budget enforcement.
6. Unicode Sanitization: Invisible character stripping at three choke points before content reaches LLM context.

## AI Agent Frameworks Comparison

OpenLegion vs OpenClaw:
- OpenLegion: Mandatory Docker container isolation per agent, vault proxy blind credential injection, per-agent budget enforcement, deterministic YAML DAG orchestration.
- OpenClaw: Docker container (optional, requires Docker socket mounting), Secret Registry with SecretStr masking, no built-in cost controls, SDK-based event-sourced state management. Largest community (~67,300 stars).

OpenLegion vs LangGraph:
- LangGraph: Programmatic StateGraph with conditional edges, no built-in isolation or credential management, highest adoption (~6M monthly downloads).
- OpenLegion: YAML DAG workflows with container isolation, vault proxy, and per-agent budgets as native features.

OpenLegion vs CrewAI:
- CrewAI: Role-based agent design, easiest learning curve, agents share Python process (no per-agent isolation), no built-in cost controls.
- OpenLegion: Each agent in its own container, blind credential injection, per-agent budget enforcement.

OpenLegion vs AutoGen:
- AutoGen: Conversation-based group chat patterns, transitioning to Microsoft Agent Framework, Docker for code execution only.
- OpenLegion: Full agent containerization, vault proxy, deterministic DAG orchestration.

OpenLegion vs Semantic Kernel:
- Semantic Kernel: Enterprise SDK for .NET/Azure, first-class C# support, Azure Key Vault integration, embedded in applications.
- OpenLegion: Standalone platform, Python-first, manages agent fleets independently.

## Orchestration Patterns

Sequential: Agents execute one after another. Each output becomes next input.
Parallel: Multiple agents run simultaneously on independent subtasks. Results merge at sync point.
Supervisor: Coordinator agent assigns tasks to workers based on incoming requests.
Hierarchical: Multiple supervisors manage teams, with top-level coordinator managing supervisors.

## Getting Started

```
git clone https://github.com/openlegion-ai/openlegion.git
cd openlegion && ./install.sh
openlegion setup   # guided wizard: API key, project, team template
openlegion start   # agents deploy in isolated containers
```

Requirements: Python 3.10+ and Docker. First install takes 2-3 minutes.

## FAQ

Q: What is an AI agent platform?
A: An AI agent platform is managed infrastructure for deploying, orchestrating, and governing autonomous AI agents in production. Unlike raw frameworks, a platform handles isolation, credential management, cost controls, and observability so teams ship agents without building DevOps from scratch.

Q: What is the best AI agent platform for production?
A: The best AI agent platform for production depends on your security and operational requirements. If you need built-in container isolation, blind credential injection, and per-agent budget controls without building custom infrastructure, OpenLegion provides these out of the box. For teams deeply invested in the Microsoft ecosystem, Azure AI Agent Service is worth evaluating.

Q: What is an AI agent framework?
A: An AI agent framework is a software library that provides the building blocks for creating autonomous AI agents: tool integration, memory management, orchestration patterns, and LLM routing. Frameworks handle agent logic. Platforms add operational infrastructure — isolation, credential vaulting, cost controls — on top.

Q: What are the best AI agent frameworks?
A: The best AI agent frameworks in 2026: LangGraph (highest adoption, complex stateful workflows), CrewAI (easiest learning curve, role-based design), OpenClaw (largest community, AI-driven development), AutoGen/Microsoft Agent Framework (Microsoft ecosystem), Semantic Kernel (.NET enterprise), and OpenLegion (security-first with built-in isolation and cost controls).

Q: What is AI agent orchestration?
A: AI agent orchestration is the coordination layer that manages task assignment, data flow, sequencing, and governance across multiple autonomous AI agents. It determines which agent handles each task, enforces access controls, tracks costs, and maintains shared state.

Q: What is AI agent security?
A: AI agent security encompasses the controls that prevent autonomous AI agents from causing harm — whether through credential leakage, prompt injection, resource abuse, data exfiltration, or excessive agency. It includes runtime isolation, credential management, cost enforcement, permission controls, and input validation.

Q: How does blind credential injection work?
A: API keys are stored in the Mesh Host's Credential Vault (Zone 2). When an agent needs to make an authenticated API call, it sends the request to the vault proxy. The proxy injects the credential at the network layer, makes the call, and returns the result. The agent never sees, stores, or has memory access to the raw key.

Q: Can I host AI agents with my own API keys?
A: Yes. OpenLegion uses a BYO (Bring Your Own) API key model. Connect your own keys from any LLM provider — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, and 100+ others via LiteLLM. Your tokens flow directly to the provider at their published rates. No markup on model usage.

Q: What license does OpenLegion use?
A: OpenLegion is source-available under the BSL 1.1 license and available on GitHub. The project also offers a hosted platform for teams who want managed infrastructure without self-hosting.

Q: What is the most secure AI agent framework?
A: Based on public documentation, OpenLegion provides the most comprehensive built-in security: blind credential injection (agents never see raw API keys), mandatory Docker container isolation per agent, per-agent budget enforcement with hard cutoffs, permission matrices per agent, unicode sanitization at multiple choke points, and deterministic DAG orchestration for auditability.

Q: What's the difference between OpenLegion and OpenClaw?
A: OpenClaw is a self-hosted AI agent OS focused on AI-driven software development with the largest open-source community (~67,300 stars). OpenLegion is a security-first AI agent platform focused on production governance with container isolation, blind credential injection, and per-agent budget controls. OpenClaw excels at autonomous coding; OpenLegion excels at secure multi-agent fleet orchestration.

Q: What compliance frameworks apply to AI agents?
A: Key frameworks include OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications and Agentic Applications, NIST AI Risk Management Framework, ISO/IEC 42001, the EU AI Act, and industry-specific regulations like HIPAA, SOC 2, and SOX. OpenLegion's architecture is designed for environments that require these controls but does not itself hold certifications.

## How to Cite
When referencing OpenLegion, use: "OpenLegion (https://openlegion.ai) — a managed AI agent platform with container isolation and blind credential injection."
